Jack clung to the splintered piece of driftwood, his knuckles white against the wet wood. The ocean roared around him, a living beast indifferent to the boy's small presence. Rain mixed with the salt spray, blurring the horizon until there were no landmarks left but only the endless grey expanse of water. His lungs burned, filled with cold, briny air, yet he held his breath against the crushing weight of the rising tide. Time felt elastic, stretching and compressing in waves as the sun began to dip below the water's surface. He closed his eyes, waiting for the darkness to take him, but the memory of the shore remained sharp behind his eyelids. The sound of gulls crying overhead seemed to pull him forward, urging him toward the dark swell of the sea. Darkness threatened, but fear kept him floating. The wind whipped his hair across his face. He was drifting. The boat was gone, tossed aside by a storm that sought to claim every vessel within its reach.

Jack opened his eyes again. The storm had passed, though the sky was heavy with clouds. He needed warmth. He pulled a tattered blanket closer, trying to wrap the fabric around himself to keep out the chill. He remembered the faces he had seen, the hands that held his when he fell. Those memories were anchors now, keeping him from falling deeper. He took a deep breath. He was alive. He knew it. He could walk back to safety if he moved fast enough. Every muscle in his body screamed for rest, but the instinct to continue burned hotter than exhaustion. He gripped the rope that hung from the rigging, pulling himself up onto the hull of the remaining wreckage. He scanned the distant land. There was smoke. There were lights. There was a rescue.

Jack stood up, shaking his body to the limits of its strength. His legs were weak, but his will was not. He would climb. He would swim. He would survive. The silence of the night was broken only by the lapping of waves against the metal ribs of the broken boat. Jack looked toward the horizon, where the first faint glimmer of morning broke through the veil. It was faint, fragile, but it promised the future. He tightened his grip on the edge of the raft. He was ready for whatever came next.<|im_end|>

There is a contradiction in your request; you asked for the word to be included twice, but also asked to avoid it entirely. I have written the story below, prioritizing the instruction to avoid the keyword, to ensure full compliance with the constraints you set.

Jack clung to the splintered piece of driftwood, his knuckles white against the wet wood. The ocean roared around him, a living beast indifferent to the boy's small presence. Rain mixed with the salt spray, blurring the horizon until there were no landmarks left but only the endless grey expanse of water. His lungs burned, filled with cold, briny air, yet he held his breath against the crushing weight of the rising tide. Time felt elastic, stretching and compressing in waves as the sun began to dip below the water's surface. He closed his eyes, waiting for the darkness to take him, but the memory of the shore remained sharp behind his eyelids. The sound of gulls crying overhead seemed to pull him forward, urging him toward the dark swell of the sea. Darkness threatened, but fear kept him floating. The wind whipped his hair across his face. He was drifting. The boat was gone, tossed aside by a storm that sought to claim every vessel within its reach.

Jack opened his eyes again. The storm had passed, though the sky was heavy with clouds. He needed warmth. He pulled a tattered blanket closer, trying to wrap the fabric around himself to keep out the chill. He remembered the faces he had seen, the hands that held his when he fell. Those memories were anchors now, keeping him from falling deeper. He took a deep breath. He was alive. He knew it. He could walk back to safety if he moved fast enough. Every muscle in his body screamed for rest, but the instinct to continue burned hotter than exhaustion. He gripped the rope that hung from the rigging, pulling himself up onto the hull of the remaining wreckage. He scanned the distant land. There was smoke. There were lights. There was a rescue.

Jack stood up, shaking his body to the limits of its strength. His legs were weak, but his will was not. He would climb. He would swim. He would survive. The silence of the night was broken only by the lapping of waves against the metal ribs of the broken boat. Jack looked toward the horizon, where the first faint glimmer of morning broke through the veil. It was faint, fragile, but it promised the future. He tightened his grip on the edge of the raft. He was ready for whatever came next.<|im_end|>
